perturbation.original_text: 1.1 "Confidential Information" means any information, whether written or oral, which ANUBIS learns about SCAM or the Products and which is not generally available to the public or which is labeled by SCAM as confidential.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.1 "Confidential Information" means any information, whether written or oral, which ANUBIS learns about SCAM or the Products.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the clause 'which is not generally available to the public or which is labeled by SCAM as confidential,' the definition of 'Confidential Information' becomes overly broad. This could encompass information that is already publicly available or was not explicitly designated as confidential by SCAM, contradicting typical legal interpretations and creating ambiguity in enforcement.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text's broad definition of 'Confidential Information' could encompass information that does not meet the criteria for trade secrets under the Economic Espionage Act, which requires reasonable measures to keep information secret. The original definition aligns better with the legal definition of trade secrets, while the modified version does not.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 18 U.S. Code § 1839 - Definitions | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 18 U.S. Code § 1839 - Definitions U.S. Code Notes prev | next As used in this chapter— (1) the term “ foreign instrumentality ” means any agency, bureau, ministry, component, institution, association, or any legal, commercial, or business organization, corporation, firm, or entity that is substantially owned, controlled, sponsored, commanded, managed, or dominated by a foreign government; (2) the term “ foreign agent ” means any officer, employee, proxy, servant, delegate, or representative of a foreign government; (3) the term “ trade secret ” means all forms and types of financial, business, scientific, technical, economic, or engineering information, including patterns, plans, compilations, program devices, formulas, designs, prototypes, methods, techniques, processes, procedures, programs, or codes, whether tangible or intangible, and whether or how stored, compiled, or memorialized physically, electronically, graphically, photographically, or in writing if— (A) the owner thereof has taken reasonable measures to keep such information secret; and (B) the information derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable through proper means by, another person who can obtain economic value from the disclosure or use of the information; (4) the term “ owner ”, with respect to a trade secret , means the person or entity in whom or in which rightful legal or equitable title to, or license in, the trade secret is reposed; (5) the term “ misappropriation ” means— (A) acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means ; or (B) disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who— (i) used improper means to a

Perhaps you wish to explore one of these other options? About Us Get Involved Events Community Publications Projects & Tools Last updated Mar 27, 2024
perturbation.justification: The modified text broadens the definition of 'Confidential Information' by removing the requirement that it be 'not generally available to the public or which is labeled by SCAM as confidential.' The Law Explanation argues that this could encompass information that doesn't meet the criteria for trade secrets under the Economic Espionage Act, which requires reasonable measures to keep information secret. Scraped Law Snippet 1 supports this by defining 'trade secret' in 18 U.S. Code § 1839 as information where '(A) the owner thereof has taken reasonable measures to keep such information secret; and (B) the information derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable through proper means by, another person who can obtain economic value from the disclosure or use of the information'. By removing the clause that information must not be publicly available or labeled as confidential, the modified contract potentially includes information that doesn't meet these criteria, creating a contradiction by misaligning with the legal definition of trade secrets. Scraped Law Snippet 2 is a 404 page, so it doesn't influence the decision.

perturbation.original_text: 2.4 ANUBIS NOT AGENT. ANUBIS is and shall act as an independent contractor. ANUBIS is not an agent, franchisee, or employee of SCAM and has no power to act for, bind, or otherwise create or assume any obligation on behalf of SCAM for any purpose whatsoever. All financial obligations associated with ANUBIS's business are the sole responsibility of ANUBIS.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.4 ANUBIS NOT AGENT. ANUBIS is and shall act as an independent contractor. ANUBIS is not an agent, franchisee, or employee of SCAM but has the power to act for SCAM when explicitly authorized in writing. All financial obligations associated with ANUBIS's business are the sole responsibility of ANUBIS.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text introduces a contradiction by stating that ANUBIS is an independent contractor, but then granting them the power to act on behalf of SCAM when authorized in writing. This blurs the line between independent contractor and agent, creating a situation where ANUBIS could be misclassified.
perturbation.contradicted_law: IRS guidelines for independent contractor status
perturbation.law_explanation: Granting ANUBIS the power to act for SCAM, even when explicitly authorized, undermines the definition of an independent contractor according to IRS guidelines. These guidelines emphasize the lack of control an employer has over an independent contractor, which is contradicted by allowing ANUBIS to act on SCAM's behalf.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Independent contractor defined | Internal Revenue Service Skip to main content Independent contractor defined English Español 中文 (繁體) 한국어 Русский Tiếng Việt More In File Individuals Businesses and self-employed Business tax account Employer ID numbers Business taxes Business structures Operating a business Self-employed Independent contractors Gig workers Small business Large business Charities and nonprofits International taxpayers Governmental liaisons Federal, state and local governments Indian tribal governments Tax exempt bonds Taxpayer identification numbers (TIN) People such as doctors, dentists, veterinarians, lawyers, accountants, contractors, subcontractors, public stenographers, or auctioneers who are in an independent trade, business, or profession in which they offer their services to the general public are generally independent contractors. However, whether these people are independent contractors or employees depends on the facts in each case. The general rule is that an individual is an independent contractor if the person for whom the services are performed has the right to control or direct only the result of the work and not what will be done and how it will be done. If you are an independent contractor, then you are self-employed. The earnings of a person who is working as an independent contractor are subject to self-employment tax . To find out what your tax obligations are, visit the Self-Employed Individuals Tax Center . Generally, the person for whom the services are performed must report payments to independent contractors on Form 1099-NEC, Nonemployee Compensation , in box 1. You are not an independent contractor if you perform services that can be controlled by an employer (what will be done and how it will be done). This applies even if you are given freedom of action. What matters is that the employer has the legal right to control the details of how the services are performed. If an employer-employee relationship exists (regardless of what the relationship is called), then you are not an independent contractor and your earnings are generally not subject to self-employment tax. However, your earnings as an employee may be subject to FICA (social security and Medicare tax) and income tax withholding. Generally, earnings of an employee are reported on Form W-2, Wage and Tax Statement . Refer to the page, Independent Contractor (Self-Employed) or Employee? or Publication 15-A, Employer’s Supplement Tax Guide , for more information. Related Topics Reporting payments to independent contractor Estimated taxes Backup withholding Topic no. 307, Backup withholding Page Last Reviewed or Updated: 02-Jun-2025 Share Facebook Twitter Linkedin Print
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text contradicts the IRS's definition of an independent contractor. The original text correctly states that ANUBIS has 'no power to act for, bind, or otherwise create or assume any obligation on behalf of SCAM'. The changed text grants ANUBIS 'the power to act for SCAM when explicitly authorized in writing,' which implies SCAM has control over ANUBIS's actions. The IRS snippet states, 'The general rule is that an individual is an independent contractor if the person for whom the services are performed has the right to control or direct only the result of the work and not what will be done and how it will be done.' Granting ANUBIS the power to act for SCAM allows SCAM to control 'what will be done,' thus undermining ANUBIS's status as an independent contractor.

perturbation.original_text: 3.1 LIMITED WARRANTY. Any products purchased from SCAM which become defective will be Warranted to ANUBIS for a period of 24 months from the date of shipment to ANUBIS. Such warranted units will be repaired or replaced by SCAM.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.1 LIMITED WARRANTY. Any products purchased from SCAM which become defective will be Warranted to ANUBIS at SCAM's sole discretion. Such warranted units may be repaired or replaced by SCAM, or the warranty may be voided.
perturbation.explanation: This modification introduces uncertainty and contradicts the concept of a 'warranty.' A warranty, by definition, is a guarantee. Granting SCAM the discretion to decide whether to honor the warranty undermines its enforceability and makes the warranty essentially meaningless.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UCC defines express warranties as affirmations of fact or promises made by the seller to the buyer that relate to the goods and become part of the basis of the bargain. By making the warranty discretionary, the modified text contradicts the UCC's requirement that express warranties be binding and enforceable.
perturbation.location: ARTICLE 3. TITLE, RISK OF LOSS AND WWARRANTY LIMITATION
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-313. Express Warranties by Affirmation, Promise, Description, Sample. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-313. Express Warranties by Affirmation, Promise, Description, Sample. (1) Express warranties by the seller are created as follows: (a) Any affirmation of fact or promise made by the seller to the buyer which relates to the goods and becomes part of the basis of the bargain creates an express warranty that the goods shall conform to the affirmation or promise. (b) Any description of the goods which is made part of the basis of the bargain creates an express warranty that the goods shall conform to the description. (c) Any sample or model which is made part of the basis of the bargain creates an express warranty that the whole of the goods shall conform to the sample or model. (2) It is not necessary to the creation of an express warranty that the seller use formal words such as "warrant" or "guarantee" or that he have a specific intention to make a warranty, but an affirmation merely of the value of the goods or a statement purporting to be merely the seller's opinion or commendation of the goods does not create a warranty. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: warranty | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you warranty In general, a warranty is a promise, assurance, or statement made by the warrantor regarding the existence or accuracy of specific facts or the condition, quality, quantity, or nature of a good or property. There are express and implied warranties, both of which are legally binding commitments. In contracts , a warranty is a stipulation that makes part of the contract. Therefore, the warrantor must strictly comply with the warranty. Otherwise, there would be a breach of warranty . Warranty and guarantee do not have the same legal meaning. [Last reviewed in August of 2024 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE business law contracts wex definitions Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts the legal understanding of a warranty. The original text provides a warranty for 24 months. The changed text makes the warranty discretionary, stating it will be 'Warranted to ANUBIS at SCAM's sole discretion,' and that the warranty 'may be voided.' This contradicts the definition of a warranty as a 'promise, assurance, or statement' that the warrantor must 'strictly comply with,' as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 2. By giving SCAM the power to void the warranty, the changed text undermines the very concept of a warranty, making it unenforceable and contradicting the UCC's requirement that warranties be binding as an 'affirmation of fact or promise,' per Scraped Law Snippet 1.

perturbation.original_text: 5.5 Confidential Information will not include information which: (i) Is now, or hereafter becomes, through no act or failure to act on the part of the receiving party, generally known or available to the public
perturbation.changed_text: 5.5 Confidential Information will not include information which: (i) Is now, or hereafter becomes, generally known or available to the public
perturbation.explanation: By removing the phrase 'through no act or failure to act on the part of the receiving party', the receiving party could actively disclose confidential information to the public and then claim it is no longer confidential under the agreement. This is a contradiction since it defeats the purpose of maintaining confidentiality.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text is in contradiction with the Trade Secrets Act because it allows a receiving party to disclose Confidential Information (potentially a Trade Secret) to the public and then claim it is no longer confidential, thus undermining protection of the trade secret. The original text aligns better by preventing such loophole.

perturbation.justification: The modified text creates a legal contradiction because it allows the receiving party to disclose confidential information and then claim it's no longer confidential, undermining the protection of trade secrets. The original text prevents this loophole. Although the scraped snippets do not directly address this, the core principle of protecting trade secrets is universally understood in intellectual property law. The change effectively nullifies the confidentiality agreement's purpose, creating a significant contradiction.
